result.markdown:
--- title: "Session 5: 4-State Markov Model — Chronic Kidney Disease" subtitle: "Early screening and ACE-inhibitor intervention vs standard care" format: html: toc: true code-fold: show code-tools: true --- ## The Clinical Question Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects approximately 17% of the Indian population, with about 6% having stage 3 or worse. Most patients in India present late — over 50% are first seen when eGFR is already below 15 ml/min (stage 5), by which point dialysis or transplantation is needed at enormous cost. **The HTA question:** Is early population-based screening for CKD followed by ACE-inhibitor therapy cost-effective compared to standard care (where CKD is detected only when symptomatic or incidental)? This is a **Markov cohort model** — the most common model type for chronic diseases that progress through stages over time. ## What Is a Markov Model? A Markov model tracks a **cohort** of patients through defined **health states** over multiple **time cycles** (usually years). At each cycle, patients can: - Stay in their current state - Move to a worse state (progression) - Move to a better state (rare in CKD, but possible with treatment) - Die The movement between states is governed by **transition probabilities**. These probabilities are the heart of the model. ## Our 4-State Model ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(DiagrammeR) grViz(" digraph ckd_markov { graph [rankdir=LR, bgcolor='transparent', fontname='Helvetica', nodesep=0.8] node [fontname='Helvetica', fontsize=11, style='filled,rounded', shape=box] E [label='Early CKD\n(Stage 1-2)\neGFR ≥ 60', fillcolor='#59a14f', fontcolor='white', width=1.8] M [label='Moderate CKD\n(Stage 3)\neGFR 30-59', fillcolor='#f28e2b', fontcolor='white', width=1.8] A [label='Advanced CKD\n/ Dialysis\n(Stage 4-5)', fillcolor='#e15759', fontcolor='white', width=1.8] D [label='Death', fillcolor='#76b7b2', fontcolor='white', shape=doublecircle, width=1.2] E -> M [label='p_12', fontsize=10] M -> A [label='p_23', fontsize=10] E -> D [label='p_1d', fontsize=10] M -> D [label='p_2d', fontsize=10] A -> D [label='p_3d', fontsize=10] E -> E [label='stay', style=dashed, fontsize=9] M -> M [label='stay', style=dashed, fontsize=9] A -> A [label='stay', style=dashed, fontsize=9] } ") ``` ::: {#fig-ckd-states .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` CKD Markov model: health states and transitions ::: ::: The four states are: 1. **Early CKD (Stage 1-2):** eGFR ≥60, often asymptomatic. Managed with lifestyle modification and renoprotective drugs. 2. **Moderate CKD (Stage 3):** eGFR 30-59. Requires specialist referral, medication optimisation. 3. **Advanced CKD / Dialysis (Stage 4-5):** eGFR <30. Requires dialysis or transplantation. 4. **Death:** Absorbing state — patients cannot leave. ## Model Parameters ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # ============================================================ # MODEL PARAMETERS — CKD Markov Model # ============================================================ # --- Cohort --- n_cohort <- 10000 # Cohort of 10,000 patients with early CKD n_cycles <- 20 # 20-year time horizon cycle_length <- 1 # Annual cycles # --- Starting Distribution --- # All patients start in Early CKD (detected through screening) state_names <- c("Early CKD", "Moderate CKD", "Advanced/Dialysis", "Death") n_states <- length(state_names) # --- Annual Transition Probabilities: STANDARD CARE --- # Source: Adapted from international CKD progression literature # (Go et al. 2004 NEJM; Kerr et al. 2012; SEEK study India) # Adjusted for Indian demographics (younger onset, higher diabetes burden) # From Early CKD (Stage 1-2) p_12_standard <- 0.10 # Early → Moderate (annual) p_1d_standard <- 0.02 # Early → Death (background + CKD-related) # From Moderate CKD (Stage 3) p_23_standard <- 0.12 # Moderate → Advanced (annual) p_2d_standard <- 0.04 # Moderate → Death # From Advanced CKD / Dialysis (Stage 4-5) p_3d_standard <- 0.15 # Advanced → Death (annual) # Source: Indian dialysis mortality ~15-25% annually # (Rajapurkar et al. 2012; Indian CKD Registry) # --- Transition Probabilities: SCREENING + ACE-INHIBITOR --- # ACE-inhibitor reduces progression by ~34% (HR 0.66) # Source: ACC/AHA meta-analysis 2024; Hou et al. 2006 NEJM treatment_effect_hr <- 0.66 # Hazard ratio for progression p_12_intervention <- 1 - (1 - p_12_standard)^treatment_effect_hr p_23_intervention <- 1 - (1 - p_23_standard)^treatment_effect_hr # Mortality not directly affected by ACE-inhibitor in advanced CKD p_1d_intervention <- p_1d_standard p_2d_intervention <- p_2d_standard p_3d_intervention <- p_3d_standard cat("=== Transition Probabilities ===\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` === Transition Probabilities === ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Early → Moderate: Standard =", p_12_standard, ", Intervention =", round(p_12_intervention, 4), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Early → Moderate: Standard = 0.1 , Intervention = 0.0672 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Moderate → Advanced: Standard =", p_23_standard, ", Intervention =", round(p_23_intervention, 4), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Moderate → Advanced: Standard = 0.12 , Intervention = 0.0809 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # --- Costs (₹ per year) --- # Source: Indian hospital data; PMJAY package rates; Jeloka et al. 2007; # Inside CKD India projections (Lancet eClinicalMedicine 2024) # State costs (annual) cost_early <- 12000 # OPD visits, basic labs, lifestyle counselling cost_moderate <- 45000 # Specialist visits, medications, frequent labs cost_advanced <- 350000 # Dialysis (2-3 sessions/week at public hospital rates) cost_death <- 0 # Intervention-specific costs cost_screening <- 500 # One-time population screening cost per person cost_ace_annual <- 6000 # ACE-inhibitor + monitoring (generic enalapril/ramipril) # --- Utility Weights (annual QALYs) --- # Source: Adapted from Gorodetskaya et al. 2005; Tajima et al. 2010 # Indian EQ-5D data limited; disclaimer applies utility_early <- 0.85 utility_moderate <- 0.72 utility_advanced <- 0.55 # Dialysis significantly reduces quality of life utility_death <- 0.00 # --- Discount Rate --- discount_rate <- 0.03 # 3% annual for both costs and QALYs ``` ::: ## Building the Transition Matrices A **transition matrix** is a table where each row represents the current state and each column represents where patients can go. Each row must sum to 1.0 (everyone goes somewhere). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(knitr) # --- Standard Care Transition Matrix --- # Rows: current state, Columns: next state tm_standard <- matrix(0, nrow = n_states, ncol = n_states, dimnames = list(state_names, state_names)) # From Early CKD tm_standard["Early CKD", "Moderate CKD"] <- p_12_standard tm_standard["Early CKD", "Death"] <- p_1d_standard tm_standard["Early CKD", "Early CKD"] <- 1 - p_12_standard - p_1d_standard # From Moderate CKD tm_standard["Moderate CKD", "Advanced/Dialysis"] <- p_23_standard tm_standard["Moderate CKD", "Death"] <- p_2d_standard tm_standard["Moderate CKD", "Moderate CKD"] <- 1 - p_23_standard - p_2d_standard # From Advanced/Dialysis tm_standard["Advanced/Dialysis", "Death"] <- p_3d_standard tm_standard["Advanced/Dialysis", "Advanced/Dialysis"] <- 1 - p_3d_standard # Death is absorbing tm_standard["Death", "Death"] <- 1 cat("=== Standard Care Transition Matrix ===\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` === Standard Care Transition Matrix === ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} kable(tm_standard, digits = 4, caption = "Annual transition probabilities — Standard Care") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Annual transition probabilities — Standard Care | | Early CKD| Moderate CKD| Advanced/Dialysis| Death| |:-----------------|---------:|------------:|-----------------:|-----:| |Early CKD | 0.88| 0.10| 0.00| 0.02| |Moderate CKD | 0.00| 0.84| 0.12| 0.04| |Advanced/Dialysis | 0.00| 0.00| 0.85| 0.15| |Death | 0.00| 0.00| 0.00| 1.00| ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- Intervention Transition Matrix --- tm_intervention <- matrix(0, nrow = n_states, ncol = n_states, dimnames = list(state_names, state_names)) tm_intervention["Early CKD", "Moderate CKD"] <- p_12_intervention tm_intervention["Early CKD", "Death"] <- p_1d_intervention tm_intervention["Early CKD", "Early CKD"] <- 1 - p_12_intervention - p_1d_intervention tm_intervention["Moderate CKD", "Advanced/Dialysis"] <- p_23_intervention tm_intervention["Moderate CKD", "Death"] <- p_2d_intervention tm_intervention["Moderate CKD", "Moderate CKD"] <- 1 - p_23_intervention - p_2d_intervention tm_intervention["Advanced/Dialysis", "Death"] <- p_3d_intervention tm_intervention["Advanced/Dialysis", "Advanced/Dialysis"] <- 1 - p_3d_intervention tm_intervention["Death", "Death"] <- 1 cat("=== Intervention Transition Matrix ===\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` === Intervention Transition Matrix === ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} kable(tm_intervention, digits = 4, caption = "Annual transition probabilities — Screening + ACE-inhibitor") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Annual transition probabilities — Screening + ACE-inhibitor | | Early CKD| Moderate CKD| Advanced/Dialysis| Death| |:-----------------|---------:|------------:|-----------------:|-----:| |Early CKD | 0.9128| 0.0672| 0.0000| 0.02| |Moderate CKD | 0.0000| 0.8791| 0.0809| 0.04| |Advanced/Dialysis | 0.0000| 0.0000| 0.8500| 0.15| |Death | 0.0000| 0.0000| 0.0000| 1.00| ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Key Insight: The Matrices Notice how the two matrices differ only in the progression probabilities (Early→Moderate and Moderate→Advanced). The treatment effect slows progression but does not change mortality directly. This is a realistic representation of what ACE-inhibitors do — they buy time, keeping patients in less costly, higher-quality health states for longer. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyr) library(ggplot2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Prepare data for heatmap tm_df_std <- as.data.frame(as.table(tm_standard)) names(tm_df_std) <- c("From", "To", "Probability") tm_df_std$Strategy <- "Standard Care" tm_df_int <- as.data.frame(as.table(tm_intervention)) names(tm_df_int) <- c("From", "To", "Probability") tm_df_int$Strategy <- "Screening + ACE-inhibitor" tm_combined <- rbind(tm_df_std, tm_df_int) ggplot(tm_combined, aes(x = To, y = From, fill = Probability)) + geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) + geom_text(aes(label = round(Probability, 3)), size = 3) + facet_wrap(~Strategy) + scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#e15759") + labs(title = "Transition Probability Heatmaps", subtitle = "Darker = higher probability of transition") + theme_minimal() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8), legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Transition matrix heatmap — Standard Care vs Intervention](ckd-markov-model_files/figure-html/fig-tm-heatmap-1.png){#fig-tm-heatmap width=672} ::: ::: ## Running the Markov Model Now we simulate the cohort moving through states over 20 years: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- Function to run a Markov cohort simulation --- run_markov <- function(tm, n_cohort, n_cycles, state_names, costs, utilities, discount_rate, initial_state = 1) { n_states <- length(state_names) # Trace matrix: rows = cycles, columns = states # Records how many people are in each state at each cycle trace <- matrix(0, nrow = n_cycles + 1, ncol = n_states, dimnames = list(paste0("Cycle_", 0:n_cycles), state_names)) # Everyone starts in the initial state trace[1, initial_state] <- n_cohort # Run the simulation for (cycle in 1:n_cycles) { trace[cycle + 1, ] <- trace[cycle, ] %*% tm } # --- Half-Cycle Correction --- # Average the proportion in each state between start and end of each cycle # This is more accurate than assuming everyone transitions at cycle start/end trace_hcc <- (trace[1:n_cycles, ] + trace[2:(n_cycles + 1), ]) / 2 # --- Calculate Costs and QALYs per Cycle --- discount_factors <- 1 / (1 + discount_rate)^(0:(n_cycles - 1)) cycle_costs <- trace_hcc %*% costs cycle_qalys <- trace_hcc %*% utilities # Apply discounting discounted_costs <- cycle_costs * discount_factors discounted_qalys <- cycle_qalys * discount_factors list( trace = trace, trace_hcc = trace_hcc, total_cost = sum(discounted_costs), total_qaly = sum(discounted_qalys), cycle_costs = discounted_costs, cycle_qalys = discounted_qalys ) } # Define cost and utility vectors costs_standard <- c(cost_early, cost_moderate, cost_advanced, cost_death) costs_intervention <- c(cost_early + cost_ace_annual, # ACE-inhibitor added cost_moderate + cost_ace_annual, cost_advanced, # ACE-inhibitor usually stopped in advanced CKD cost_death) utilities <- c(utility_early, utility_moderate, utility_advanced, utility_death) # --- Run Both Arms --- results_standard <- run_markov(tm_standard, n_cohort, n_cycles, state_names, costs_standard, utilities, discount_rate) # Intervention includes one-time screening cost results_intervention <- run_markov(tm_intervention, n_cohort, n_cycles, state_names, costs_intervention, utilities, discount_rate) # Add one-time screening cost (undiscounted, occurs at time 0) results_intervention$total_cost <- results_intervention$total_cost + n_cohort * cost_screening cat("=== Markov Model Results (20-year horizon) ===\n\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` === Markov Model Results (20-year horizon) === ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("STANDARD CARE:\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` STANDARD CARE: ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Total cost: ₹", format(round(results_standard$total_cost), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total cost: ₹ 8,065,724,874 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Total QALYs:", round(results_standard$total_qaly, 1), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total QALYs: 84707.5 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Cost per patient: ₹", format(round(results_standard$total_cost / n_cohort), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Cost per patient: ₹ 806,572 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" QALYs per patient:", round(results_standard$total_qaly / n_cohort, 3), "\n\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` QALYs per patient: 8.471 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("SCREENING + ACE-INHIBITOR:\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` SCREENING + ACE-INHIBITOR: ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Total cost: ₹", format(round(results_intervention$total_cost), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total cost: ₹ 6,473,703,963 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Total QALYs:", round(results_intervention$total_qaly, 1), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total QALYs: 92356.5 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" Cost per patient: ₹", format(round(results_intervention$total_cost / n_cohort), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Cost per patient: ₹ 647,370 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat(" QALYs per patient:", round(results_intervention$total_qaly / n_cohort, 3), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` QALYs per patient: 9.236 ``` ::: ::: ## ICER Calculation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} inc_cost <- results_intervention$total_cost - results_standard$total_cost inc_qaly <- results_intervention$total_qaly - results_standard$total_qaly icer <- inc_cost / inc_qaly cat("=== Incremental Cost-Effectiveness ===\n\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` === Incremental Cost-Effectiveness === ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Incremental cost: ₹", format(round(inc_cost), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Incremental cost: ₹ -1,592,020,911 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Incremental QALYs:", round(inc_qaly, 1), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Incremental QALYs: 7649.1 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("ICER: ₹", format(round(icer), big.mark = ","), "per QALY gained\n\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` ICER: ₹ -208,132 per QALY gained ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Interpretation wtp_india <- 170000 # ~1x GDP per capita if (icer < 0) { cat("→ Intervention is DOMINANT (less costly, more effective)\n") } else if (icer < wtp_india) { cat("→ Highly cost-effective (ICER < 1x GDP per capita)\n") } else if (icer < 3 * wtp_india) { cat("→ Cost-effective (ICER < 3x GDP per capita)\n") } else { cat("→ NOT cost-effective at conventional thresholds\n") } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` → Intervention is DOMINANT (less costly, more effective) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} qaly_data <- data.frame( Strategy = c("Standard Care", "Screening +\nACE-inhibitor"), Total_QALY = c(results_standard$total_qaly / n_cohort, results_intervention$total_qaly / n_cohort), Total_Cost = c(results_standard$total_cost / n_cohort, results_intervention$total_cost / n_cohort) ) library(gridExtra) p1 <- ggplot(qaly_data, aes(x = Strategy, y = Total_QALY, fill = Strategy)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) + geom_text(aes(label = round(Total_QALY, 2)), vjust = -0.5, size = 4) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#e15759", "#4e79a7")) + labs(x = "", y = "QALYs per Patient", title = "Discounted QALYs") + theme_minimal() p2 <- ggplot(qaly_data, aes(x = Strategy, y = Total_Cost / 1000, fill = Strategy)) + geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) + geom_text(aes(label = paste0("₹", format(round(Total_Cost), big.mark=","))), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#e15759", "#4e79a7")) + labs(x = "", y = "Cost per Patient (₹ thousands)", title = "Discounted Costs") + theme_minimal() grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Discounted QALYs breakdown by strategy](ckd-markov-model_files/figure-html/fig-qaly-breakdown-1.png){#fig-qaly-breakdown width=672} ::: ::: ## Visualising the Markov Trace The **Markov trace** shows how the cohort distributes across health states over time: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ggplot2) library(tidyr) # Prepare trace data for plotting trace_df_std <- as.data.frame(results_standard$trace / n_cohort) trace_df_std$Cycle <- 0:n_cycles trace_df_std$Strategy <- "Standard Care" trace_df_int <- as.data.frame(results_intervention$trace / n_cohort) trace_df_int$Cycle <- 0:n_cycles trace_df_int$Strategy <- "Screening + ACE-inhibitor" trace_combined <- rbind(trace_df_std, trace_df_int) trace_long <- pivot_longer(trace_combined, cols = all_of(state_names), names_to = "State", values_to = "Proportion") trace_long$State <- factor(trace_long$State, levels = state_names) ggplot(trace_long, aes(x = Cycle, y = Proportion, fill = State)) + geom_area(alpha = 0.8) + facet_wrap(~Strategy) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("Early CKD" = "#59a14f", "Moderate CKD" = "#f28e2b", "Advanced/Dialysis" = "#e15759", "Death" = "#76b7b2")) + labs( x = "Year", y = "Proportion of Cohort", title = "Markov Trace: CKD Progression Over 20 Years", subtitle = "Comparing standard care vs early screening + ACE-inhibitor" ) + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Markov trace: cohort distribution over 20 years](ckd-markov-model_files/figure-html/markov-trace-plot-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Reading the Markov Trace In these plots, the green area (Early CKD) shrinks over time as patients progress to worse states. The intervention keeps more patients in green for longer — that is the visual signature of a treatment that slows disease progression. Notice how the red area (Advanced/Dialysis) grows more slowly in the intervention arm. Since dialysis is by far the most expensive state (₹3,50,000/year), this delay translates directly into cost savings. ::: ## Patients Reaching Dialysis ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dialysis_data <- data.frame( Year = rep(0:n_cycles, 2), Proportion = c(results_standard$trace[, "Advanced/Dialysis"] / n_cohort, results_intervention$trace[, "Advanced/Dialysis"] / n_cohort), Strategy = rep(c("Standard Care", "Screening + ACE-inhibitor"), each = n_cycles + 1) ) ggplot(dialysis_data, aes(x = Year, y = Proportion * 100, colour = Strategy)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + labs( x = "Year", y = "% of Cohort on Dialysis", title = "Patients Reaching Advanced CKD / Dialysis", subtitle = "Early intervention delays progression to this costly state" ) + scale_colour_manual(values = c("Standard Care" = "#e15759", "Screening + ACE-inhibitor" = "#4e79a7")) + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Proportion of cohort in Advanced CKD/Dialysis over time](ckd-markov-model_files/figure-html/dialysis-comparison-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Cost Accumulation Over Time ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cumcost_std <- cumsum(results_standard$cycle_costs) / n_cohort cumcost_int <- cumsum(results_intervention$cycle_costs) / n_cohort # Add screening cost to intervention cumcost_int <- cumcost_int + cost_screening cost_over_time <- data.frame( Year = rep(1:n_cycles, 2), Cumulative_Cost = c(cumcost_std, cumcost_int), Strategy = rep(c("Standard Care", "Screening + ACE-inhibitor"), each = n_cycles) ) ggplot(cost_over_time, aes(x = Year, y = Cumulative_Cost / 1000, colour = Strategy)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + labs( x = "Year", y = "Cumulative Cost per Patient (₹ thousands)", title = "Cost Accumulation: Standard Care vs Intervention", subtitle = "Intervention costs more early but may save through avoided dialysis" ) + scale_colour_manual(values = c("Standard Care" = "#e15759", "Screening + ACE-inhibitor" = "#4e79a7")) + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Cumulative discounted costs per patient over 20 years](ckd-markov-model_files/figure-html/cumulative-costs-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## What You Just Did You built and ran a complete **Markov cohort model** — one of the most common model types in HTA worldwide. The key concepts were: 1. **Health states** — the distinct conditions patients can be in 2. **Transition probabilities** — the likelihood of moving between states each cycle 3. **Transition matrix** — the mathematical structure encoding all movements 4. **Markov trace** — tracking the cohort distribution over time 5. **Half-cycle correction** — a technical adjustment for more accurate cost/QALY estimation 6. **Discounting** — accounting for time preference in costs and outcomes In Excel, building this model requires complex array formulas or VBA macros, and extending it to 20 cycles across 4 states becomes unwieldy. In R, the core simulation is a single line: `trace[cycle + 1, ] <- trace[cycle, ] %*% tm` — matrix multiplication handles all the transitions at once. ## Key References - Go AS et al. (2004). Chronic kidney disease and the risks of death and hospitalisation. *NEJM*. - Singh AK et al. (2013). Epidemiology and risk factors of CKD in India — SEEK study. *BMC Nephrology*. - Rajapurkar MM et al. (2012). CKD in India: a clarion call for change. *Lancet*. - ACC/AHA (2024). Risk of initiating ACEi/ARBs in advanced CKD. - Inside CKD Investigators (2024). Economic burden of CKD at the patient level. *Lancet eClinicalMedicine*. - Hou FF et al. (2006). ACE inhibitor in progressive renal disease. *NEJM*. - Indian CKD Registry. BMC Nephrology 2012. → **Next:** Open `ckd-exercise.qmd` to explore how changing parameters affects the model.
